summary: I was born in Myanmar in 1999, in a place called Ge Lo. My birth happened during a very difficult time because my family was running from the Burmese government due to war and persecution. The area was surrounded by mountains, and we had to hide from soldiers, which made my early life very unsafe.

In 2003, my family moved to the Mae Surin Refugee Camp in Thailand to escape the civil war in Burma. We built our house there with bamboo and leaves, and although the camp provided shelter and education, life was still hard with limited freedom and food shortages. I lived there for eight years and went to school with my siblings, but we couldn't leave the camp or find jobs.

Thailand was where my family found refuge after fleeing Myanmar in 2003. We lived in the Mae Surin Refugee Camp there, which gave us shelter and some education despite the hardships. It was a place of safety compared to where we came from, but we still faced restrictions like not being allowed to leave the camp or work.

In March 2012, my family and I arrived in Cleveland, Ohio after a long journey through several countries and airports. Cleveland was a beautiful city with a nice downtown, and I started school there at Thomas Jefferson High School. I lived in Cleveland for two years before moving to Lakewood because housing was expensive and there was a lot of violence where we lived.

In November 2014, my family moved to Saint Paul, Minnesota because we heard there were many Karen people there who could help us. I attended Washington Technology Magnet School after we moved. Living in Saint Paul gave me better opportunities and a community where my family felt more supported.

My family decided to immigrate to the United States in 2012 because we heard it offered better education and more opportunities. After passing interviews and tests, we arrived in Cleveland, Ohio, and began a new life. Living in the U.S. has allowed me to access education and opportunities I never had before, and I am very thankful for that.

When I first came to Cleveland in 2012, I attended Thomas Jefferson High School starting in 8th grade in 2013. It was a challenging experience because I didn’t know English and struggled to understand my classmates, who were mostly Spanish and Black. The teachers were kind and encouraging, which helped me a lot during those difficult early days.

After two years in Cleveland, my family moved to Lakewood, Ohio because housing was cheaper and the area was safer. I went to Lakewood High School and was in 10th grade. Being the only Asian student in my class was hard, and I remember feeling isolated and overwhelmed, especially in classes like world history where I sometimes didn’t understand the lessons.

At Lakewood High School, where I attended 10th grade, I was often the only Asian student in my classes. It was difficult for me to keep up, especially since I was still learning English. I remember one time in world history class when I was asked a question I didn’t understand, and I started to cry because I felt so lost.

When my family moved to Saint Paul, Minnesota in 2014, I started attending Washington Technology Magnet School. This school gave me better educational opportunities and a supportive environment. I am grateful to my family for bringing me to the United States where I can pursue my dreams with more freedom and resources.
